title: Five Years of TEDxCortina — My End of Beginning
content:
I ended my role at TEDxCortina in September 2025, but I waited until early December to write this—back where it started, at my parents' place in Auronzo di Cadore. Closing the loop matters to me, and this mountain house is where I first hit send on my application in 2020. That song *End of Beginning* by DJO keeps looping in my head: you realise a chapter lived all the way through, and the only thing left is to wave at the end of beginning. Sitting here with the same Dolomite view I had as a kid, I can feel the five-year arc compress into a single breath.
--- ## Five years distilled What I'm taking with me. There are so many things that i will always keep with me from this experience, but the one i think could be helpful for everybody are: I joined with an email titled *I'm not what you're looking for*. They needed logistics skills; I had ten years of competitive sailing, zero logistics lines on my CV, and a lot of enthusiasm. I started in the Speaker Team as the youngest one adding names to spreadsheets. The organisers trusted me to tinker, and I began building internal tools so teams could work faster. That was the first lesson: if you solve boring problems, people let you keep solving bigger ones.
![Logistics role cover](/five-years-of-tedxcortina/cover-role.png) My role boiled down to eleven quiet months and one superhero month. During event week, I was the person who made everything possible from a logistics point of view—deliveries, materials, speakers, emergencies, and a few miracles. One year I rode the ski-lift up and down so many times that I felt like I'd climbed Everest by sunset. It taught me that endurance and calm beat any Gantt chart when the clock is ticking. Life did its full-circle thing: I became Logistics Team Leader—the job I thought I wasn't qualified for. Maybe the best qualification was simpler: owning a Vito van. If you have a Vito, you are logistics. The van was more than transport; it was a rolling Swiss Army knife, a meeting room, and sometimes a therapy couch for stressed speakers. Step by step, I ended up representing TEDxCortina at international events—something unimaginable on day one. - Verona for TEDxWeekend, standing with other team leads in front of TED leadership. One of the highlights of my TEDx run. - Ischia for the Italian Gathering, where I fell in love with Naples, the island, and the people I met. ## Stories you only get by being there Some memories live rent-free for years. The most cinematic one: I got my van—Benzo—completely stuck in the mud at the fanciest golf club in Cortina, right during the gala dinner. Picture tuxedos, heels, champagne... and me outside, digging a van out of the fairway. There are many more, but they'd take days to write. There was also the midnight sprint to a hardware store for missing cables, the impromptu stage repair with duct tape and faith, and the sunrise coffee shared with volunteers who had zero energy left but endless heart. ## The chapter that grew with me TEDxCortina evolved into a more sophisticated event every year, and my role expanded with it—from moving boxes to orchestrating an ecosystem. At some point I knew it was time to sail toward a new adventure. The more polished the event became, the more I realised I loved the scrappy early days and the feeling of building something out of nothing. --- ## What mattered most The best part was always the team. Pre-event tension, the build-up, the magic of event day—those moments changed me. Leaving was one of the hardest decisions I've made. We grew together by trusting each other with tough calls and showing up when things got messy. I keep a small card that says 'Kekko TVB,' written by someone who believed in me more than I believed in myself. It reminds me that TEDx is about people who lift you up. I learned that sincere encouragement can unlock more potential than any process diagram. TEDxCortina also gave me something priceless: I met my current co-founder at Amperry there, along with extraordinary people who pushed me forward and never limited me. The community became a radar for good humans; it still guides who I want to build with. ## What I'm taking with me - How to manage people and support their work. - How vital clear, honest, constant communication is. - How to spot the people who lift you up and expand your potential. - The surprise that many people are even better in real life than from afar—and some are not. - That logistics is just another word for care: making sure people have what they need, when they need it. These lessons come with me into every new chapter. --- ## Turning the page Every good story has the moment you turn the page, even when you're not ready. This is mine. Now it's time to sail toward new projects, with the same passion TEDxCortina helped me discover. The stage lights fade, but the habit of building with purpose stays on. As the song says: *You take the man out the city, not the city out the man.* As for my journer: i will be no longher part of TEDxCortina, but TEDxCortina will never leave me. Thank you to everybody that was part of it.
excerpt: Closing the loop on five years with TEDxCortina—logistics, road trips, lessons, and the people who made it unforgettable.
